name: System Design — Fundamentals
description: Interview framework, foundational concepts, vocabulary. The vocabulary every system-design conversation assumes.

cards.definition: 1) Requirements (5 min — functional, non-functional, out of scope) → 2) Estimation (3-5 min) → 3) API Design (3-5 min) → 4) High-level design (10 min) → 5) Deep dive on 2-3 hard problems (15-20 min) → 6) Bottlenecks & failure modes (5 min).
cards.example: Spend 2 min clarifying requirements before drawing — saves 10 min of rework.

cards.term: Key habits in a system-design interview
cards.definition: State tradeoffs explicitly (don't just name a technology). Drive the conversation — propose, check in. Clarify before drawing. Use numbers — '10M DAU, 100 writes/sec' justifies sharding.
A transaction fully succeeds or fully fails. No partial writes visible after a crash mid-transaction.
Data always moves from one valid state to another. Constraints, foreign keys, and invariants are preserved across transactions.
Concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other. Implemented via locking or MVCC; tunable via isolation levels (read uncommitted → serializable).
Once a transaction is committed, the data survives crashes — typically by writing to a write-ahead log before acknowledging.
Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent. Trades consistency for availability and performance — appropriate for social-media likes, not bank balances.

During a network partition, a distributed system must choose between Consistency (every read sees the latest write) and Availability (every request gets a response). Partitions are inevitable, so the real choice is CP vs AP under partition.
Extends CAP for normal operation: even without a Partition (P), there's a tradeoff between Latency (L) and Consistency (C). Reading from a local replica is faster but may be slightly stale.
cards.example: Spanner = CP/EC. Cassandra = AP/EL. MongoDB default = CP/EL.

cards.definition: Every read sees the most recent write; all clients see the same data at the same time. Costly — usually requires consensus or quorum reads (linearizable systems).
Given no new updates, all replicas converge to the same value. Replicas may temporarily disagree. Acceptable for likes/cart totals/DNS; not for balances/inventory.
The strongest single-object consistency model: every operation appears to take effect atomically at some point between its start and end, in real-time order. What 'strongly consistent' usually means.

An operation produces the same result no matter how many times it runs. Critical for retry-on-failure systems. SET x=5 is idempotent; INCREMENT x is not. HTTP GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent; POST is not.
A unique key (UUID) the client sends with non-naturally-idempotent operations. Server stores (key, result); on retry, returns the cached result instead of re-executing. Prevents double-charges.
cards.example: Stripe API: clients pass an Idempotency-Key header on payment creation.

cards.definition: Servers hold no per-client state between requests; all state lives in DB/cache/external store. Any instance can handle any request → trivially horizontally scalable behind a load balancer.
Vertical = bigger box (more CPU/RAM). Horizontal = more boxes. Vertical hits hardware ceilings and is a SPOF; horizontal scales further but needs the system to be stateless or partitioned.
A component whose failure stops the entire system. The first thing to identify in a design review. Mitigations: redundancy, leader election with failover, multi-AZ deployment.
Designing for minimal downtime. Typically expressed in 9s: 99.9% = ~8.7h/yr, 99.99% = ~52 min/yr, 99.999% = ~5.3 min/yr. Each 9 is ~10× harder.
When the active instance dies, traffic is redirected to a standby. Active-passive: standby idles until promotion. Active-active: both serve traffic and the dead one is removed.
Latency = time to complete one request (p50/p95/p99). Throughput = requests per second. Optimizations often trade them: batching raises throughput at the cost of latency.
p99 / p99.9 latencies. With 100+ downstream calls per request, the worst-case becomes the typical-case — at fan-out 100 with p99=100ms, ~63% of requests see ≥100ms somewhere.

Before modifying data on disk, the change is appended to a sequential log. On crash, the log is replayed for durability. Cheap (sequential writes), enables async data-page flushing, and is the substrate for streaming replication.

Partitioning (sharding): one logical dataset split across nodes serving the same app. Federation: different domains in different databases (users DB, orders DB, inventory DB). Federation removes cross-DB joins.

2PC: coordinator locks all participants then commits/aborts globally. Strong consistency, blocking, coordinator is SPOF. Saga: sequence of local txns with compensating actions on failure. Eventually consistent, no locks, harder to reason about.
In Dynamo-style replication with N replicas, choose write quorum W and read quorum R such that R + W > N. Then any read overlaps with the most recent write — latest data is always seen.
Rough sizing in an interview: derive QPS from DAU × actions/day / 86,400; storage from records × size; bandwidth from QPS × payload. Round up to the next power of 10. Justifies design choices.
cards.example: 10M DAU × 100 actions/day / 86400 ≈ 12k QPS average → design for ~25k peak.

cards.definition: Content Delivery Network — edge nodes geographically close to users that cache static (and some dynamic) content from an origin. Reduces latency and origin load. Behavior controlled by HTTP cache headers.

Distributes requests across backend instances. L4 (TCP) is faster but opaque to HTTP semantics; L7 (HTTP) can route by URL/header/cookie and do TLS termination. Common algorithms: round-robin, least-connections, consistent-hash.

cards.term: Common bottlenecks to check at the end
cards.definition: Database (writes hit a single primary?), single hot key (one user/object getting all traffic?), thundering herd on cache miss, network egress between regions, queue backups indicating downstream slowness, and any single coordinator (leader/dispatcher).

After the design works at the stated scale, ask 'what breaks at 10×?' Usually: a single DB primary, cache memory, a hot shard, a sync API call that needs to become async, or a coordinator that becomes a SPOF.
